comments.title: Astrology
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,
Blessings to you and my fellow commentators! My questions for this week’s edition of Magic Mondays revolve around the subject of astrology.
1. In my natal chart, Mercury Square Mars. My Mars is also in detriment in Taurus. I know I’ll have to do planetary charity to both planets. I would like to ask, what is an appropriate charity for Mars? I started doing research and came across a local organization that provides financial support to military orphans or children of fallen soldiers. Would this suffice?
2. My Mercury is in Aquarius. According to some, Mercury is exalted in Aquarius. Others disagree, saying it is exalted in Virgo. What is your take on this?
3. I have a bit of apprehension of doing planetary charity with Mars, He is the God of War and the Lesser Malefic, after all; even though I know that this devotional act of magic is safe. Upon further reflection, I realize that this is something I need to do. I am guilty of people-pleasing (especially before), peace at any cost/avoidant personality, and have had my kindness taken advantage of. Methinks that doing this planetary charity could be the beginning of bringing in beneficial Mars energies that will balance out aspects of my personality.

Thank you again for hosting Magic Mondays.

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content:
Hey JeanLuc,

As a similar Mars in Taurus and Mars square Mercury, I've done planetary charity to veteran's organizations and a dog charity to good effect. While Mars may be a malefic, that doesn't necessarily means it's a bad energy, just challenging at times. I found the charity to be of great help dealing with conflict avoidance, sloth and a bad temper so I'd highly recommend you follow through with this plan of action.

Tamanous

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content:
Mars is afflicted in my chart, too. And yes, planetary charity annually has smoothed my rough edges noticeably.

OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content: Thanks, Tamanous. This is encouraging.

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content:
1) Planetary charity for Mars can be directed toward soldiers or the children or orphans of soldiers, yes, as well as livestock, which Mars rules, and to wolves and other Martial animals.

2) Traditionally Mercury is exalted in Virgo and in no particular dignity in Aquarius. I've always found this to be the case in my own practice.

3) Mars is harsh but he's also fair. Give him an honest offering and you'll get an honest and commensurate response. My Mars is also afflicted, but I've found that working with him constructively has been very helpful for me.

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content:
Oooh, interesting.

On this topic, I saw on the Mundane Astrology essay comments that you place Neptune in exaltation in Gemini, different from most modern astrologers I've seen. If you wouldn't mind telling me, in your system (I think you mentioned Ivy Goldstein and a Welsh sounding guy's name?) what is Uranus' exaltation and Neptune's fall?

Also, do you consider sign placement alone when doing planetary charity, or just dignity in general? I also have Mars in Taurus (lol), but it has three excellent aspects and a great house, so I've always assumed it has enough dignity to get by.

Thirdly, what is your thoughts about the relative relationship of Mercury and Venus to literature in Mundane Astrology? I saw that unfortunately, the 2020 Grand Mutation has a so-so Venus, sad for a literature and art lover like me. However, the Mercury is in worse shape, if I remember correctly. Which do you think will have the greater impact on the next 200-odd years of literature? Do you consider them to have equal weight in such matters, given that one rules writing and the other art?

Much thanks, for your Mundane Astrology work, and for the ceaseless moderation here.

-Derpherder

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content:
1) Yes, Ivy Goldstein-Jacobson and Llewellyn George are my go-to authorities on astrology, as their methods allow accurate predictions -- something most modern astrologers either don't or can't do. Ivy's book Foundation of the Astrological Chart gives Scorpio as the exaltation of Uranus and Sagittarius as the fall of Neptune. (Remember that the detriment is always the opposite sign from the rulership and the fall is always the opposite sign from the exaltation.)

2) The whole picture must always, always, always be taken into account. The best way to do lousy astrology is to fixate on single factors. A planet debilitated by sign but dignified by aspect is always of intermediate dignity; not only do you have to judge its strength on a case by case basis, the planet that's aspecting it and the houses that planet rules and is located in also have to be factored in, as they tell you what helps prop up the natural weakness of the debilitated planet.

3) If it's art, it's Venus. If it's information, it's Mercury. If it's both, it has shared rulership. Also, watch the rulers of the 3rd and 5th houses, which rule media and the arts respectively!

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content:
Super interesting! I knew about the opposite houses thing, but was unsure if it transcended all systems. Thanks for the confirmation!

Honestly, Gemini and Sag make a lot more sense to me than Leo/Cancer and Capricorn like many moderns put as the exaltation and fall. The activeness, exuberance, and masculinity of Leo feels not quite right, Cancer is too absorbed in its own shell (lol) and Capricorn can be very self sacrificing and amenable to cosmic unity, so long as it's part of the plan! (My brother is Cap sun, Cap asc, with Saturn trine the asc, and Cap sun rising, so I know Capricorn!)

Alright, I think my Mars is just fine then given all context. The aspects are with 2 of the 3 strongest planets in my chart.

Oof. Well I hope the Neptune in Pisces at least can give some help to fantasy, my favorite genre of literature, over the next 200 or so years. Looks like it's going to need it. Uranus in Taurus does not bode well for scifi either. That being said, I note with interest the presence of Mercury in Capricorn in that chart; perhaps very conservative, sober, and grounded literature will accord best with the spirit of the times. This makes some sense in a time of collapsing economies, given that conservative politics and practicality are suited for fall just as liberal ones and idealism are for rise. Perhaps the idealism of the future will be almost solely religious (Neptune) and spiritual (Pisces) rather than political.

Much to think about, JMG. As always, Im extremely impressed by your balance of air and earth, intellectual fluidity and common sense. As real food crises bear down on us, I will take solace in books, banishing, and the stars, all thanks to your instruction. Much luck to you and Sarah in these troubled times, though perhaps with your skills you don't need it!

-Derpherder

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content: I expect fantasy fiction to thrive; with Neptune in Pisces, that whole end of culture will be extremely robust, and Venus is weak but not heavily afflicted, so the arts generally will be in better shape than they've been in since 1842. Science fiction is another matter, since it's basically backed itself into a corner by betting everything on an interstellar future that's not going to happen. Sucks, but there it is. Thanks for the good wishes; Sara and I are doing very well, actually, but that's largely because we foresaw the mess and, ahem, collapsed well ahead of the rush. I wish you excellent fortune in doing the same!

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content: Thank you, Archdruid. I bought a red candle and am ready to begin!

comments.title: Western Feng Shui and Scrying Outside of a Grove/Temple
comments.content:
Good evening!

Thanks very much for hosting these, as always, and I hope Spring is getting into full swing in your corner of the continent.

Before my questions, something to share: Winifred Hodge Rose has released her second volume of Heathen Soul Lore, Heathen Soul Lore Volume II: A Personal Approach, which is the practical exercises to Volume I's theoretical grounding. I just ordered my copy today, and it'll likely be a while before I start in on these practices with an otherwise full occult plate, but I'm very interested to give these the time they deserve after I work my way through my current commitments, and I hope some others here might find them helpful.

As for questions, two this evening:

1) Is scrying without opening a Grove/Temple an actually bad idea, or just a less-than-ideal one?

2) I've been listening to some old Podcasts with you on them, and in one or two, you talked about how your first book on Geomancy got ineptly marketed as "Western Feng Shui", which has got me wondering about what might actually qualify for that label. You've talked a bit about some Western parallels to Eastern traditions that we only have tiny scraps of (energetic aspects to martial arts, energetic healing, and so on), and so I wonder, besides the fertility-related stuff you talk about in The Secret of the Temple, do we have any hints of western approaches to arranging spaces for luck/prosperity/happiness? Or, perhaps more to the point, do you have any recommendations for resources on "Feng Shui or Feng Shui-like approaches", whether Western or Eastern, that are compatible with Western occult practices?

Thanks for your time and attention, and hosting this community,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Western Feng Shui and Scrying Outside of a Grove/Temple
replies.content:
0) I'll be talking about Winifred's latest book as soon as I have the chance to read my copy!

1) It's less than ideal, but it's workable.

2) The authentic Western traditions I know of focus almost entirely on the correct positioning, orientation, and geometry of sacred places; Nigel Pennick has several good books on that subject. Feng shui wasn't about "arranging spaces for luck" until it got dumbed down by several generations of talented marketers, you know...

replies.title: Re: Western Feng Shui and Scrying Outside of a Grove/Temple
replies.content:
2) Fair enough! I see that my question implied a concern with the marketer-flavored version of Feng Shui that I didn't intend. I'm actually more interested in what orientation/arrangement/decoration and so forth can do for making non-ritual/sacred spaces healthy or otherwise positive, without defining "positive" as "good material outcomes". Perhaps the right answer is "treat more spaces as sacred", but my understanding of sacred is that it implies "set apart for a specific holy purpose", and so normal, day-to-day living spaces wouldn't necessarily qualify.

This particular line of questions was spurred by getting a few houseplants for the first time since I've kept my own house, and immediately noticing an improvement to the spaces they're in, and wondering how to productively follow up.

replies.title: Re: Western Feng Shui and Scrying Outside of a Grove/Temple
replies.content: Experiment and keep detailed notes!

I've always been comfortable with natural magic but find rituals intimidating. I recently sought help from a deity and wish to thank her with an offering of fruit. What protocol do you use to set up an offering space - can it be done by someone who doesn't practice rituals? And what do you do with the offering afterward? The answer that seemed obvious was to bring the fruit outside after a while but I don't want to attract scavengers.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Any action that has meaning is a ritual. Making an offering to a deity is a ritual all by itself. Most people who say they don't like rituals have an overly formal sense of what ritual is or can be. If you want to make an offering to a deity, start by praying to the deity, letting them know that you want to make an offering and asking how they'd like it to be done. Then listen inwardly for the response, and act on what you perceive. The same thing goes for what to do with the offerings.

2) There I can't help you -- my background in Celtic lore is Welsh, Cornish and Breton. Anyone else?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
A quick suggestion with regard to the second question - the recent work Ireland's Immortals: A History of the Gods of Irish Myth by Mark Williams might be worth a look. It's academically very solid (and gets the thumbs up from Ronald Hutton, among others), but the author also has a background in contemporary British paganism and Jungian psychology, I believe.

Early Irish Myths and Sagas (translated by Jeffrey Gantz) is a dependable starting point for anyone who's interested in getting to grips with the early material. The Táin is another important text and is available in several translations.

Owain D.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's surprisingly difficult to find good, useful information about the Irish Gods. I would recommend starting with Morgan Daimler's "Gods and Goddesses of Ireland: a guide to Irish deities." It's very slim but pithy, part of the Pagan Portals line by Moon Books. Morgan also has volumes on specific deities, including Brigid, Manannan, the Dagda, and the Morrigan, all of which are worth reading.

--Sister Crow

replies.title: (no subject)

Homeopathy was discovered by Samuel Hahnemann in 1796, not much more than a decade after the discovery of Uranus and well before Neptune was thought of, and so the timing suggests it's Uranian, not Neptunian. That's why it's so important in homeopathic practice to find the simillimum, the one unique medicine that best fits the full symptom picture of the patient. Alternative lifestyles and philosophies are Uranian, too -- Uranus is the planet of eccentricity and going your own way, remember.

I think it's quite possible that homeopathy will see another robust revival in the decades ahead, as the corporate-medicine model of the mainstream is busy shooting itself in body parts far more painful than feet. I've been trying to contribute to that, as it happens, by discussing the eccentric offshoot of homeopathy I use, Schuessler's biochemic cell salts. comments.title: Astrology/planetary charity questions
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I am have been practicing the DMH material for a little while now and have just begun to study astrology. I have been working largely out of Lilly’s Christian Astrology, and I have been perusing Christopher Warnock’s website. I have a few questions. Hopefully it’s not too much for one post!

1) Are there other books on natal/horary astrology that I should check out?

2) My natal chart has the Moon in Capricorn square Mars in Libra which seems kind of doubly inauspicious since not only are they square, they are both in their Detriment. I think I remember reading in a past Magic Monday that when planets are square or in opposition, you can do planetary charity for both of them. Would this be a good idea in this circumstance, or does the fact that they are both in Detriment change this? I was thinking of doing charity for 5 Tuesdays for Mars and 9 rounds for the Moon. Should I wait to complete charity for the Moon before starting charity for Mars, or is it ok to do charity for the Moon on Monday and then the next day, Tuesday, do charity for Mars, and repeat this for 5 weeks (and then finish the last 4 weeks for the Moon)?

3) Maybe you’ve answered this before, but I couldn’t find it in a search of past Magic Mondays. What are some good charity ideas for Moon? What about Mars?

4) I also have Neptune in Capricorn. Some sources say that is Fall, but others say, no, that’s Aquarius. What’s your take? If Neptune in Capricorn IS in its fall, is planetary charity with “new” planets a thing? Also, in my case, Neptune is in retrograde. What day, hour, and charity would I use?

5) I am trying to better understand peregrine planets. According to Lilly, these seem quite bad, but it appears that many modern, pop astrologers consider it to be more or less neutral. What are your thoughts/experiences?

Thanks for giving us these great opportunities to pick your brain! They are truly appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Astrology/planetary charity questions
replies.content:
1) I'm a major fan of Ivy Goldstein-Jacobson's astrological writings -- her Simplified Horary Astrology is the book that taught me how to make accurate horary predictions, and she also has some very good books on natal astrology. Llewellyn George is also great.

2) You can certainly do planetary charity for afflicted planets, no matter how they're afflicted, and as far as I know there's no reason not to do them concurrently.

3) The Moon rules childbirth, the ocean, and mental illness. Mars rules war, livestock, and wolves. If you can't come up with a charity from each of those lists, you're not trying hard. ;-)

4) The system I use gives Sagittarius as the fall of Neptune. Retrogradation is no big deal; does your natal Neptune have any other afflictions?

5) I find them a debility, but a minor one.

replies.title: Re: Astrology/planetary charity questions
replies.content:
1) I'll get those right away

4) Well its square with North Node in Libra. I don't really know how to interpret that since my understanding is that the effect of the North Node varies based on whether the planet is benefic of malefic. Maybe those books would answer that. I don't have the term, triplicity, decanate or face for any of the non-traditional planets, so I don't really know. Would those books have that info for newer planets? I suspect its peregrine, but I really have no way of knowing that for sure at this point

Thanks for your help!

replies.title: Re: Astrology/planetary charity questions
replies.content:
The faces, terms, and triplicities for the recent planets haven't been worked out yet. Square north node is very minor, so I'd say treat your Neptune as though it's of middle dignity, just a little toward the debility side.

If you want to do planetary charity, that's a real challenge, as here again the details haven't been worked out yet. You could try doing it on a date that works out numerologically to 9, and choose a charity that relates to Neptune's rulerships -- substance abuse, fantasy fiction, mysticism, etc. -- but it would be experimental work.

comments.title: Menopausal magic
comments.content: Good morning from England and as always thank you for all you do. I read your FAQ again today (had to smile at how VERY simple and declarative you've recently edited certain sections to read). I noticed there that you said that magical energy is the same as sexual energy. So it makes sense to wait until after puberty to start practicing ceremonial magic. But what about the other end? Are there any effects, blockages, disabilities, etc, connected to menopause, for instance? I appreciate this may not be something you know about nor may potentially even be interested in, but if any commenters have any thoughts I would be very grateful to hear them. In particular, I hope for some reassurance that the ability to practice effective ceremonial magic does not disappear together with one's reproductive capacity and/or decrease as your libido does! Conversely, since I am myself recently entered both into menopause and magic I could keep record, provide data points if there is any interest?

replies.title: Re: Menopausal magic
replies.content:
Magical energy is the same as sexual energy, but that doesn't mean that magic begins at puberty and ends at menopause -- quite the opposite. The energies of the cosmos flow through you, and through every living being; at puberty, a certain portion of those energies are specialized for the purpose of reproduction; at menopause, that specialization ends, and those energies are free for other uses. (This is true for men, too, though the "male menopause" is a slower and subtler process.) That's why it was the old woman down the lane who people in traditional village culture sought out to get spells cast -- she no longer has a chunk of her energies set aside for the possibility of pregnancy, childbirth, and nursing, so she can put all her power into magic.

It's quite common, in fact, for women to become interested in magic when they hit menopause, or to have a previous interest suddenly shift into overdrive. As someone who's started menopause and magic together, you're in a grand tradition!

More or less as soon as I went into menopause my magical interest and capacity grew exponentially. Point being, in my experience there does seem to be some sort of strong biological link or correlation between menopause/andropause and magical capacity.

comments.title: The Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Egg (LBRE, pronounced Libre, Spanish for Free)
comments.content:
Good morning JMG and commentariat!

I learned a cleansing/banishing ritual this week using an egg after a series of what the heck's took me to a Traditional Mexican Medicine weekend class. It seems it is a common practice in America and some parts of Europe and Asia too but the version I learned comes from the Mexica (pronounced Meh-SHEE-kah) line of tradicional medicine. I've written up a detailed post after this comment went too big, I've put it up in here:

The Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Egg

I was surprised to see that it works very well! According to lore it is particularly good at getting rid of curses and evil eye. Here are some instructions in case anyone wants to attempt it. Paired with a hoodoo bath and an LRP in banishing mode I feel unstoppable. *cue SIA*

In general I feel the egg reaches deeper within but doesn't work as powerfully on the aura so I guess it works for denser ethers; nor does it leave a protective film as the hoodoo bath that lasts after ceremony. I've been working with an acupuncturist to put an end to a blockage/void in my head and the egg did something there, it felt it was sucking out stuff from my body and some points along the troublesome meridians (gall-bladder and san jiao) became active.

Of course the opening invocation can be adapted to any pantheon, and in fact I would like to hear recommendations if anyone is interested in coming up with their own and share it. This one is the one most used in Mexico using Archangel Michael and God. I'm working out one with the local pantheon but I haven't had the chance to ask my teacher yet what she thinks, Quetzalcoatl is a good option as he battled the night stars for the sun to rise up again victoriously at dawn.

Here is the opening invocation:
"I petition thee Lord Almighty, by your divine grace and the intervention of Archangel Michael that my roads may open, that all negativity and noxious energies be banished from within me and that they be absorbed by this egg. May the ill wishes, hexes, curses, bindings, psychic attacks and obstacles disappear far away from me where nobody will be harmed by them even well after their time of disintegration. May it be so, in the name of God. Amen."

replies.title: Re: Partners in the woods
replies.content: Most Druid seasonal rituals are traditionally open to everybody, whether or not they're initiates. It used to be quite common, in fact, to hold them in public places and invite anyone interested to join the circle and take part. As for what newcomers can do, it depends entirely on what they want to do. Back in the day, they could always join in repeating prayers and chants, and might be asked to help purify the grove with one or two of the elements in the opening, etc. Of course they can drink from the Hirlas and receive any other gift that's handed out.

replies.title: Re: Faerie/nature spirits

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Certainly not medical advice, but narcolepsy is often associated with insomnia and sleep deprivation, as you might expect.

Some other things for the OP to look for: do you have hypnagogic hallucinations (vivid sensory experiences and/or seemingly lucid dreams as you fall asleep)? When you do quiet, non-physically active things like reading a book, do you find yourself nodding off?

If any of those ring true, one possibility to consider is to sharply limit your caffeine intake. I thought I had sleep onset insomnia for years, but it turns out I'm a slow caffeine metabolizer. This means that I don't get as noticeable a rush from caffeine as some people do, but it stays in my system longer, which manifested as me not feeling like late afternoon caffeinated beverages were affecting me, but then not being able to fall asleep. I now generally follow a "no caffeine after 2:00 pm and no more than one cup of coffee a day" rule and I have a much easier time falling asleep.

Hope that gives you a few avenues to explore!

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/179202.html?thread=29001218#cmt29001218

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology and non-ritual magic
replies.content:
1) That's for serious astrologers. Synastry -- the art of relating two or more astrological charts -- is a fairly advanced technique. At your level, you might glance back over the older predictions from time to time, and remember that the indications in one reading don't cancel out those in another; they all have to be taken together.

2) Ceremonial, yes; magic, no. Magic is there to cause change, and most people who take it up want to cause changes in their lives -- improve their financial situation, find a loving partner, or what have you -- and they want rituals to do that. The kind of occultism I'm teaching in the Golden Section books won't do that; it'll improve your life no end, but it does that by cultivating wisdom rather than power.

replies.content:

2. Gotcha. I’ll need to mull this over a bit more, but I guess I was just assuming that „internal change“ in the form of wisdom is also some form of change covered by magic, as that can also have external effects. But I get the distinction now - very helpful explanation.

comments.title: Magical essence

Do you know if any studies have been done on when a women gives birth part of her magical essence leaves with the child? If this is the case does the essence renew in the female over or time or could she deplete her essence by having children?
Thank you for your time and insight.

replies.title: Re: Magical essence
replies.content:
In women, part of the life force that flows through the body becomes specialized at puberty to make pregnancy and childbirth possible, and during pregnancy, birth, and nursing, a lot of the life force goes into those activities. There's a much more limited connection until the child turns seven and finishes developing a wholly independent etheric body. The thing to keep in mind is that you don't have a limited supply of the life force. The force that flows through you comes from the universe and returns to the universe; it's as close to infinite as anything created can be. Prayer and spiritual practices -- anything that opens you to the cosmos -- will recharge you with more magical force than you can imagine, and enable you to bless yourself and everyone around you with an abundance of life and love.

I am reflecting a lot on Levi's latest chapter and your commentary about this on the latest post.

I work as a low-level office fauna at a pretty large company. I don't use magic to enhance my position at work, but since I started doing a daily banishing ritual, meditation and divination, I've been more successful at work, and have had several opportunities for promotion, and even some pressure to get promoted.

The last thing I want to do is get promoted!

Because I am greatly put off by many things that seem to come with corporate life. The weird business language one is supposed to use, the strange way one relates to others there that seems separate from the way one deals with people outside of work, and especially that one has to make decisions that favour the business as opposed to the interests of employees.

Although it doesn't affect me, right now management is having to force people back into the office after two years of remote work. The move is almost universally unpopular, and the mood is terrible among affected staff. It is instructive to watch other colleagues who are managers, who themselves don't support this move, have to pretend to their reports that going back to in-office working really is a good idea.

Assuming as Levi writes that a magical chain can be formed from employees as easily as from students, my question is: If magic is concerned with the structures of consciousness, then what structure of consciousness does corporate life encourage?

There seems to be something related between physical things you find in offices, like the bad lighting, glass walls, bland grey furniture, white tables, etc, with the strange business vocabulary that people use only at work, and a mindset that focuses mainly on the "value added" and measurable things that look pretty in tables and charts.

(What's a little scary is that the type of furniture you see in offices is spreading to people's homes. I also had a friend recently send me an invite to a birthday meal as an emailed auto-generated meeting invitation that looks a lot like my meeting calendar software at work)

replies.content:
Not JMG, and not especially magical, but if I may offer a few thoughts on corporate life and business speech (I used to work as a management consultant and now I teach Business Communication at a university):

1. On getting promoted: very many corporate job paths change rather a lot as you get promoted. You start out doing some kind of specific, substantive work, but as you get promoted, you do less of that and more of other stuff, like selling or managing. Unfortunately, it is often the case that you do less of what you like about the job and more of what you don't like. Noticing this and making appropriate choices can help (for example, I left consulting due to this and some other factors).

2. Generally speaking, promotions offer you three things (besides more money): status, power, and the opportunity to develop others. Status is the social prestige you get from your new title, whether within the job, outside of it, or both. Power is your ability to make more things happen in the world (when you tell 5 people what to do, you can have a bigger impact than when you only tell yourself what to do). Developing others is when you train and teach people, answer their questions, help them sort out their own career, and so forth. Most human beings enjoy and get some pleasure out of all three, but you might discover that, say, status doesn't mean much to you, or that the kind of power that comes from managing a team at work is not the kind of power you find satisfying.

3. If you resist pushes to get promoted, be aware that some places have an "up or out" policy. Basically, their reasoning is that if you aren't doing more of what the company values (managing, selling, etc), but you still might be insisting on higher pay or greater benefits or whatever, that you are not worth keeping around. In such cultures, if you go a while without getting promoted, you might get let go. If it is that kind of culture, this might not be such a bad thing, but it's certainly better to be ready for it! Also, sometimes companies have to offer more consideration if they decide to let you go than if you quit (severance pay or the like), so that's worth including in your calculations.

4. Lastly, some resources, both of which are at the more cynical end of analyzing corporate dynamics, but which I think offer some helpful insights. I should say that working for a team that is insulated from the worse aspects of corporate life and led by a good person/boss is night and day different from the more common miserable experience. Anyhow, on to the resources:

- Zvi Mowshowitz has a lengthy review/summary of the book Moral Mazes which analyzes the perverse incentives present in corporate structure and culture. You can find a summary of the series with links if it proves interesting here: https://thezvi.wordpress.com/2020/05/23/mazes-sequence-summary/

- Venkatesh Rao has a series of blog posts called "The Gervais Principle" that uses "The Office" as an illustration of social/psychological dynamics at work, arguing that self-interested manipulators see office culture as the sham it is and figure out how to make the most of it, other people see the sham and just check out, not seeking fulfillment from work, and some buy the sham, totally commit to it, and are used as dupes by the manipulators. Again, I think it is slightly overly cynical as a stand alone theory, but it has some good insights, and the writing is pretty entertaining: https://www.ribbonfarm.com/2009/10/07/the-gervais-principle-or-the-office-according-to-the-office/

I hope something in the above helps, and good luck working out your best path!

Thanks,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Monday remarks
replies.content:
1) Prayer and planetary charity are always useful, and if you know ceremonial magic, you can invoke the spiritual/sephirothic archetypes beyond the planets, which are not affected by astrological factors. (You can invoke Geburah, for example, and get good results, when Mars is afflicted.)

2) The stars incline, they do not compel. A person with a fairly bland chart can still accomplish quite a bit in life, though the blandness will show in various ways -- it's just that if your chart isn't strong, you're going to have to work for it. As for malefic forces, of course you can use those constructively. Every malefic planet can become a source of strength -- Mars gives you courage and vigor, Saturn gives you patience and stability, Uranus gives you originality and a capacity for unexpected moves, Pluto gives you secrecy and the ability to manipulate.

3) This is a classic example of single-factor astrology, which is a great way to make bad predictions. Okay, Jupiter's in Pisces -- so? What is he aspecting in your natal chart and progressions? What is he aspecting in the foundation chart and ingress charts of the country where you live? What else is happening in the heavens at the same time? (Hint: with Uranus in his fall, there are going to be plenty of crises every time he aspects something relevant or something relevant aspects him.) Also, keep in mind that in mundane charts, Neptune is a malefic, and with Neptune in his own sign, similar factors apply. The whole sky, not just a single planetary placement, must be taken into account to make astrology work!

She had gone into a set of bookshelves in my room and removed your book on Natural Magic from its spot mixed in with other random non-occult books in a disorganized area of my shelves, then put the two books in her room set up side by side as pictured. Without trying to make a big deal about it or make her uncomfortable, I asked her why, and she said, "Because these two books go together." I asked her why they went together (to see--without giving a leading question--if she could somehow recognize JMG's name, which would have been a first) and she seemed puzzled. She struggled to answer beyond, "I know they just are supposed to go together." So I left it at that (and eventually took both books back.) If that's what happened, she'll be OK, I think. This sentient force seems to be very kind to small children who can sense it, and very patient with them. It protects them.

comments.title: confounding factors in geomancy

Just as a theoretical example, say someone has a health scare and while waiting for the medical results, casts a chart with dice for the question “does this indicate a serious illness?” and the chart shows exactly what they fear. Puella
Fortuna major
Carcer
Populous

Amissio
Populous
Fortuna minor
Cauda draconis

Laetitia
Carcer
Amissio
Albus

1. The doctor can help, but only in a limited way,
2. The doctor cannot help at all for some reason.
3. Carcer in the 3rd might suggest the illness is local (3rd house) and limited, and this is why the doctor can help.

replies.title: Re: Divination systems and their variations
replies.content: It depends on the diviner. Some people can get good results from anything, some people get better results from one thing than another. replies.title: Re: Divination systems and their variations
replies.content:
As a data point, I have wooden Rune staves and currently use cards for the Ogham.

Most of what I've noticed is different for me is more what I'd call practical differences - for example, if you literally cast (drop/throw) wooden lots on a surface, one thing you can analyze is how close together or far apart they are, which isn't a particularly practical thing to do with cards. On the other hand, when I was doing a three-Rune spread for a daily reading, I would pull the three runes out from the dice tray I dropped them into, one at a time, and lay them out to read. This method made it a bit tougher to get "reversed" Runes than with cards

Oh, and lastly, in my experience, wooden lots seem more prone to falling/jumping out "on their own", which can sometimes give some interesting insights.

replies.title: Re: Names
replies.content:
There's the classic, slightly-annoying but also effective, technique of getting someone's name for the first time, and then using their name three times in the ensuing chitchat.

I've been teaching it to my children as a game, with scoring: they pretend to have just met each other, and they score one point for each time in the ensuing silly conversation they use the other's names (Nice to meet you, Sam. So, Sam, what brings you to the area? Now, Sam, just to make sure I remember, you're from Wazoo and you're a professional hamster trainer?). Two points if they can make some inoffensive comment about the name itself (Oh, I have a cousin named Terry, or Is that Terry with a y, or Terri with an i?), Three if they can also repeat a personal detail the other person divulged at the same time. I'm hoping the rehearsing will help my socially-awkward kiddo, but in the meantime, it's a hilarious car game.

replies.title: Re: Names
replies.content:
If I might share two techniques that have helped me somewhat:

I got this one from a student, but it's not for the faint of heart: when you find yourself struggling to remember someone's name and you're talking to that person, force yourself to refer to them by your best guess of their name. The terror of getting it wrong will help motivate getting it right, and if you do get it wrong, the embarrassment of being corrected will help it to stick.

A kinder, gentler method that I worked out in college is to ask someone to confirm their name at the end of your initial interaction with them. Usually when we introduce ourselves, we know almost nothing about the other person. By the end of the conversation, you have some actual associations to make with their name ("Oh yes, John is the fellow I spoke to about the Russia-Ukraine war").

replies.title: Re: Occult and/or esoteric aspects of chess?
replies.content:
Manly P. Hall has a paragraph on this in The Secret Teachings of All Ages:

In its symbolism chess is the most significant of all games. It has been called "the royal game"—the pastime of kings. Like the Tarot cards, the chessmen represent the elements of life and philosophy. [...] The chessboard consists of 64 squares alternately black and white and symbolizes the floor of the House of the Mysteries. Upon this field of existence or thought move a number of strangely carved figures, each according to fixed law. The white king is Ormuzd; the black king, Ahriman; and upon the plains of Cosmos the great war between Light and Darkness is fought through all the ages. Of the philosophical constitution of man, the kings represent the spirit; the queens the mind; the bishops the emotions; the knights the vitality; the castles, or rooks, the physical body. The pieces upon the kings' side are positive; those upon the queens' side, negative. The pawns are the sensory impulses and perceptive faculties—the eight parts of the soul. The white king and his suite symbolize the Self and its vehicles; the black king and his retinue, the not-self—the false Ego and its legion. The game of chess thus sets forth the eternal struggle of each part of man's compound nature against the shadow of itself. The nature of each of the chessmen is revealed by the way in which it moves; geometry is the key to their interpretation. For example: The castle (the body) moves on the square; the bishop (the emotions) moves on the slant; the king, being the spirit, cannot be captured, but loses the battle when so surrounded that it cannot escape.


Hall has a knack for cramming a month of meditation themes into a paragraph, so I hope that proves an adequate starting place!

I REALLY have to get around to reading Hall. That is a paragraph as rich as the finest Italian cuisine!

comments.title: The angels and archangels

Could you explain what the angels and archangels are? Which of the planes do they live on and do they have any similar or shared goals?

replies.title: Re: The angels and archangels
replies.content:
In the occult teachings I follow these days, angels belong to a swarm of souls that completed their pilgrimage through matter in the very distant past -- before life emerged on any of the planets of this solar system -- and who now cooperate with the Solar Logos, the god of this solar system, to maintain the solar system in balance and foster the evolution of the souls (like you and me) who are still working their way through incarnation. They are conscious, individual beings, far above us in the evolutionary process though much less complex than we will be when we reach their level. (There will be swarms after ours which will be far more complex than we are, so it all balances out.)

Archangels are a little different. They, like the Solar Logos, completed their evolutionary journey long before this solar system was even formed; they are the Star Logoi discussed in the Cosmic Doctrine and the gods of the old Pagan faiths. They are far beyond the angels in evolution and in complexity. They also cooperate with the Solar Logos in the shaping of the solar system's evolution and destiny.

The angels exist naturally on the sixth or Causal Plane (that's Causal, btw, not casual!), though they can range across the planes at will. The archangels exist on the seventh or Divine Plane, and can act instantly on all planes.

comments.title: Trinitarian Doctrine
comments.content:
Please delete if this is more of a "Theology Thursday" question and not a Magic Monday question 😁

Hello all, I know this is an awfully big question, but lately I've been absolutely puzzled as to why Christians insist that Jesus must be fully God and man. Now, I do understand why Jesus must be fully man in order for his crucifixion to have its redemptive qualities (man must pay for their own evil etc.), but aside from God forming a covenant and sanctioning/ accepting the sacrifice as sufficient, I don't get why He absolutely had to be there physically on the cross. I've been grilling my Catholic boyfriend on this issue, and his reply was that the totality of mankind's sin and evil could only be atoned for by something ontologically prior (greater) than a mere human... but he and other mainline Catholics (and orthodox Christians) openly recognize the deification of the soul, or theosis, which basically says we have divine sparks within us, to borrow Luria's term. So wouldn't it follow that if man has the potential of being a quasi divine being made in the image of God, and dearly beloved by Him if we follow the Christian canon,that the sin of Adam (an initially pure human) could be undone and redeemed by a pure being of the same ontological class? I won't presume to know the mind of God, but asking mankind to take responsibility for its actions doesn't seem too out there to me.

So to restate my long winded question...why in the world does Jesus have to be fully God as well as human? I understand you're not a Christian, but wouldn't a sacrifice like this be more significant than just saying, "well we're all just disgusting little insects who can't do anything about evil, so obviously God must have been on that cross." Or am I missing some God within and God without type imagery?

replies.title: Re: Trinitarian Doctrine
replies.content: In traditional Catholic theology, God could have accepted as atonement the sacrifice of an ordinary, nondivine human being.  He could have also just forgiven, without any atonement, the sins of human beings.  This is called an imperfect atonement.  The traditional teaching is that God desired a perfect atonement, which only someone who is also fully divine could accomplish.  And since Christ is a divine person with a human nature, his human actions (faithful, life time obedience and his voluntary death on the Cross) are the human actions of God, and thus they have an infinite value.  That means they can cover all the sins that humankind could ever commit.  That's part of the "perfect atonement" that I just mentioned.  And so, since God desired a perfect, rather than imperfect, atonement, he became man as Jesus Christ.

replies.title: Re: Trinitarian Doctrine
replies.content: An additional point: in traditional Catholic theology, human beings do not have a spark of the divine within them.  They are made in the image of God in that they possess a rational soul, and the free will that it brings.  Theosis or deification occurs when the human soul enters the presence of God, and takes on some of his divine attributes.  The analogy that I've heard is that of a piece of metal placed in a fire.  The metal takes on the properties of the fire, e.g. heat, luminosity, etc., but it does not become the fire.  The human soul is the metal and God is the fire, in the traditional Catholic understanding.

replies.title: Re: Trinitarian Doctrine
replies.content: Thank you for the thoughtful reply! Is this perfect redemption perfect in the context of a complete redemption for mankind, or is it a perfect redemption and victory over all evil, human and nonhuman?

replies.title: Re: Trinitarian Doctrine
replies.content: My theology and philosophy are a bit fuzzy at this point but as I recall, the atonement is considered unlimited since it can apply to all of humanity.  It is also considered a victory over all evil, though since Catholicism teaches that demons do not change their minds, their decision to become evil is final.  Calvary is seen as the climactic battle between good and evil.  St. Paul references this in Colossians 2:9-15.  The scene described at the crucifixion, like the earthquake, the sun going dark, dead persons returning to life, etc., are apocalyptic imagery meant to reveal that the battle was underway.  This is seldom realized by many modern Christians, as many have an obsession with the Book of Revelation and the battle of Armageddon described therein.  But from the traditional perspective, the war is over and the enemy is now in retreat, to be finished off at its last stand in the area of Megiddo.

replies.title: Re: Trinitarian Doctrine
replies.content:
Anon here, I forgot to log in lol.

I'd also like to ask what 'made in the image of God' means, if not having a divine spark. I'm not saying that this makes every human a god, but grants us a certain affinity or closeness to the Divine even if we are not It. Perhaps it's not an exact comparison, but conceptually these two things seem very similar to each other

replies.title: Re: Trinitarian Doctrine
replies.content: IIRC, it is because human beings are infused with a rational soul.  That means we have intellect and will, which God, in the Catholic tradition, possesses in an analogical manner.  We resemble the creator because we can think and we can choose.  That's the gist, as it was explained to me (again, IIRC).  Catholic theology is heavily dependent on the thought of St. Thomas Aquinas.  St. Thomas was, in turn, heavily dependent on Aristotle.

replies.title: Re: Trinitarian Doctrine
replies.content:
Having just passed through Holy Week, my recent interpretation of the events runs more and more like this:
1. popular rabbi excites a crowd
2. some in the crowd assert that he could be a leader to oppose the Roman occupation
3. community leaders decide that they can take no chances with increased Roman oppression
4. Roman authorities test them, by offering to release the rabbi
5. by that point, the crowd has had a chance to consider the Roman response
6. "we'll take care of this troublemaker ourselves, thank you. Nothing to see here."

The resurrection story makes no sense (but that doesn't make it untrue).

In the centuries that followed, some will hear the story in hope of our own resurrections, while others will hear it as "but, you know, rocking the boat doesn't get you very far, even if you're the Son of God." The leadership thus has an interest in promoting the story. "Treat each other nicely, pay your taxes, keep your head down (or you could end up like That Guy)."

I can only assume that the people who designed the Novus Ordo Mass didn't want a spiritually powerful ritual. They wanted to exclude the Divine Presence, to reduce the ritual to a purely human community-building exercise. All those nuns in short skirts strumming on cheap guitars, singing "Kum ba ya" off key, were part and parcel of the same thing: replacing beauty with kitsch, dignity with absurdity, in an attempt to flee from the mysterium tremendum et fascinans and turn the Church into a nice safe social club they could control, and profit from, without bothering with inconveniences like the presence of God.

Yes, it's also possible that the Christian god deliberately turned his back on them, and even hardened their hearts in the classic manner. But I think they were also trying to turn their backs on Him.
